body.u: After leaving school, some 50 years ago, I got a job in a bakery. This was just moving bags of flour and yeast around, and learning about baking bread. I was offered a place for an apprenteshipapprenticeship at a college in baking but I turned this down, because in 2 monthsmonths' time (20th September) I was going to join the army.

I had worked at Woods for about five weeks. I would have liked to work there for another two months (until it was time to join the Army), but alas the bakery had to shut down. It had run out of funds. First all the shops shut down. Then I was made ‘redundant’.
I could of sat down all day, doing nothing, for two months, but I didn’t really like the idea.

Employment on this ‘Island’ was very low, but according to the statistics there were plenty of jobs. I didn’t know where though, I must have looked all day for two weeks, but still couldn’t find a job.
But then the firm my dad worked for got a government grant for £350,000 to help them put a railway line through their ‘yard’ as well as buying docking facilities etc…
Which was estimated at the time to cost around £1 ½ million to develop. I thought this was ridiculous as they were already £800,000 in the red!

This scheme was to create an extra 14 jobs. I got one of them. My Brother who had worked there before also got a job. So did my friend who had just lost his job he too was going into the Army with me.
I worked with {Flame cap?} until the 10th of September I stopped work because in 10 daysdays' time I was going to join the Army.
The 19th of September soon came. Colin (my friend who lives across the road) and me had our cases packed and were ready to leave. We were both going into the same regiment (REME) .

Our first 18 weeks of basic training was spent at Brompton barracks. About two - three miles from where our previous job had been. We got a 72 hour pass after weeks. My father picked us up. Then we had a four day leave six weeks in Basic Training.
Then we were posted to Corsham barracks in Wiltshire we finneshedfinished our apprenticipsapprenticeships. I got a degree in vehicle mechanics but Colin just failed.
Colin signed on for 3 years. But I signed on for 9 years. We visited many European countries (Germany, Belgium etc…) and after 3 years Colin signed on for another six.
When I was 21 I went to Ulster. At first I was scared. But our unit was lucky. We didn’t get posted to Belfast but to a border town. There was very little trouble here.

The only ‘trouble’ we had when an I.R.A. excutionexecution squad try to hide in the town. These were not given accommodation, because earlier a type of this squad had hid up in the town ad murdered about 10 young children when a car bomb went ‘off’ at a school crossing.
Two of the squad had been killed.
